# Research Design and Methodology {#sec-chapter3}

This chapter explains the philosophical approach, research strategy, and design decisions that govern the construction and evaluation of the proposed framework. It proceeds in two parts: the first covers the research design from philosophical positioning through to the evaluation approach; the second addresses the ethical and professional considerations arising from the use of operational data in a regulated financial institution.

## Research Design

This study is grounded in a pragmatist research philosophy. Pragmatism holds that the value of a knowledge claim is determined by whether the resulting artefact works in the context for which it was designed [@kellyThreePrinciplesPragmatism2020]. This position suits the present study because the objective is not to establish universal laws about technical debt, but to construct a quantitative framework useful for prioritisation decisions in a specific enterprise IT environment. Technical debt is treated here as a construct whose meaning is shaped by measurement choices rather than as a directly observable quantity [@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020]. The framework therefore grounds its estimates in operationally defined proxies and acknowledges that the resulting scores are representations, not facts.

### Research Strategy

Design Science Research (DSR) governs this study, as formalised by @hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004. DSR is appropriate here because the primary output is an artefact rather than a theory: a TOPSIS-based prioritisation framework operating on live ITSM data. The framework is designed to address the gap identified in @sec-chapter2, where no existing approach integrates service-level operational metrics for technical debt prioritisation in trading IT. @lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 confirm the broader absence of validated, quantitative prioritisation frameworks across the field; the service-level and enterprise-IT dimension of that gap is established through the synthesis in @sec-chapter2.

@hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004 specify seven guidelines for rigorous DSR, mapped to this study in @tbl-dsr-guidelines. On one side sits the problem space: the absence of a service-level TD framework. On the other sits the solution space: the TOPSIS artefact. These are connected through input knowledge, such as DORA metrics and MCDM methods, and through research processes, including indicator selection and sensitivity analysis. This structure positions the framework as prescriptive design knowledge applicable to regulated enterprise contexts.


| DSR Guideline | Implementation in this Study |
|---|---|
| Design as an artefact | The TOPSIS-based prioritisation framework |
| Problem relevance | Established in @sec-chapter2: no existing framework integrates service-level operational metrics for TD prioritisation in trading IT |
| Design evaluation | Sensitivity analysis; criterion validity against historical operational records |
| Research contributions | Novel indicator set; first application of TOPSIS to live ITSM data in financial trading IT |
| Research rigour | Systematic indicator selection; reproducible TOPSIS computation; documented normalisation |
| Design as a search process | Iterative refinement of indicator selection and weight assignment |
| Communication of research | This dissertation; practitioner-accessible scoring output |

: DSR guidelines and their implementation {#tbl-dsr-guidelines tbl-colwidths="[30,70]"}

The process follows @vombrockeIntroductionDesignScience2020's six-step model: problem identification and motivation (@sec-chapter1 and @sec-chapter2), definition of objectives (@sec-chapter2), design and development of the artefact (@sec-chapter4), demonstration, evaluation (@sec-chapter5), and communication (@sec-chapter6). The sequence is not strictly linear; indicator selection and evaluation criteria were refined iteratively as constraints in the available data emerged during development.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-dsr-grid
%%| fig-cap: "DSR Grid for the present study, adapted from vom Brocke, Hevner and Maedche (2020)"
%%| fig-width: 6

graph TD
    P["Problem<br/>Absence of a quantitative<br/>service-level TD prioritisation<br/>framework for trading IT"]
    IK["Input Knowledge<br/>TD taxonomy; DORA metrics;<br/>TOPSIS method; DSR foundations<br/>(Hevner et al., 2004)"]
    RP["Research Process<br/>Literature review;<br/>indicator selection;<br/>TOPSIS construction;<br/>sensitivity analysis"]
    KC["Key Concepts<br/>Technical debt;<br/>operational proxies;<br/>MCDM; ITSM service data"]
    DSR(["DSR Project"])
    S["Solution<br/>TOPSIS framework operating<br/>on live ServiceNow data<br/>(four DORA-derived indicators)"]
    OK["Output Knowledge<br/>Prescriptive design entity;<br/>validity of operational TD<br/>proxies in regulated IT"]

    P --> DSR
    IK --> DSR
    RP --> DSR
    KC --> DSR
    DSR --> S
    DSR --> OK
```

### Research Design

The study is structured as a single-organisation embedded case study, situated within the trading IT division of one of Switzerland's three largest universal banks. @turnbullUseCaseStudy2021 define this design as an investigation into a single instance of observable complex phenomena, constrained by clearly identifiable boundaries. Discrete units of analysis, in this case individual IT services, are examined within that bounded organisational setting.

Three factors make this design appropriate. First, the regulatory, operational, and vendor-platform characteristics specific to Swiss financial IT resist generalisation from software engineering studies conducted elsewhere. Second, access to granular ITSM records is unavailable through surveys or experiments. Third, @hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004 specify that a design artefact must be evaluated through rigorous, well-executed methods. The present study satisfies this requirement by evaluating the framework against operational data from the environment for which it was designed.

The study adopts the individual IT service as the unit of analysis, defined as a distinct configuration item in the organisation's ITSM instance. In this vendor-heavy enterprise environment, source-code access is unavailable for both outsourced components and on-premises vendor software [@ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021]. Service-level operational indicators are therefore tested as candidate proxies for technical debt prioritisation, rather than assumed as established substitudes. The study is cross-sectional, analysing a fixed observation window rather than tracking changes over time, consistent with the objective of demonstrating ranking validity at a point in time. Longitudinal evaluation is identified as a direction for future work.

### Data Sources and Collection

The framework operationalises four operational indicators as technical debt proxies, each supported by prior empirical validation. Two indicators, MTTR and change failure rate, are drawn from the DORA metrics framework, which has demonstrated validity as a proxy for delivery performance [@forsgrenAccelerate2018]. Incident frequency signals service instability that the literature associates with accumulated technical debt, as established in the synthesis in @sec-chapter2 [@detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021; @ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021]. Patch recency may serve as a proxy for infrastructure and dependency debt [@dissanayakeSoftwareSecurityPatch2022]. Static code metrics and expert cost estimates were excluded because static analysis tools are calibrated for in-house codebases and produce unreliable signals in vendor-heavy enterprise environments [@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020].

| Indicator | Definition | TD Dimension | Literature Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incident frequency | Number of P2/P3 incidents per service within the observation window, normalised by service age. The company defines P1 incidents as cases where the majority of its business is affected, which did not happen in the past 10 years. | Architectural debt | @detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021; @ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021 |
| Mean time to restore (MTTR) | Average elapsed time from incident creation to resolution, expressed in hours. | Infrastructure debt | @forsgrenAccelerate2018 |
| Change failure rate (CFR) | The proportion of changes that resulted in an unplanned incident or rollback within 72 hours of deployment. | Process debt | @forsgrenAccelerate2018 |
| Patch recency | The proportion of components within each service's dependency stack that are within a supported-version window, derived from the CMDB. | Vulnerability debt | @dissanayakeSoftwareSecurityPatch2022 |

: Mapping of operational indicators to technical debt dimensions {#tbl-indicators tbl-colwidths="[20,40,20,20]"}

Data were accessed under an agreed arrangement with the IT operations team over a twelve-month observation window. All records were anonymised at source, with service names replaced by alphanumeric identifiers and team and vendor labels removed.

### Framework Design

The analytical core is the Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), formulated by @hwangMultipleAttributeDecision1981 and validated through comprehensive reviews of its enterprise applications [@pandeyReviewTOPSISMethod2023]. TOPSIS addresses the single-criterion prioritisation limitation identified in @sec-chapter2 by ranking services according to their geometric proximity to ideal solutions after normalisation and weighting. Given a decision matrix with $m$ services and $n$ indicators, the method proceeds as follows.

The matrix is normalised using vector normalisation, which scales each value relative to the column norm to ensure comparability across indicators with different units and ranges [@pandeyReviewTOPSISMethod2023]:

$$
r_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij}^2}}
$$

A weighted normalised matrix is constructed using criterion weight $w_j$:

$$
v_{ij} = w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

The positive and negative ideal solutions are identified:

$$
A^+ = \{ v_1^+, \ldots, v_n^+ \}, \quad A^- = \{ v_1^-, \ldots, v_n^- \}
$$

Euclidean distances from each ideal are calculated:

$$
S_i^+ = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(v_{ij} - v_j^+)^2}, \quad
S_i^- = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(v_{ij} - v_j^-)^2}
$$

The relative closeness coefficient $C_i$ constitutes the final score:

$$
C_i = \frac{S_i^-}{S_i^+ + S_i^-}, \quad C_i \in [0, 1]
$$

A higher $C_i$ indicates a service closer to the worst-case operational profile, representing a higher technical debt priority. Incident frequency, MTTR, and CFR are cost criteria where lower values are preferable; patch recency is a benefit criterion where higher values are preferable.

Two weight configurations are used. A baseline equal-weight configuration ($w_j = 0.25$) serves as the reference case, in accordance with @grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019. A secondary configuration applies differential weights reflecting the higher operational salience of MTTR and CFR in a trading environment, as established in @sec-chapter2. The sensitivity of rankings to weight perturbation is assessed in @sec-chapter5.

### Evaluation Approach

The framework is evaluated against a single criterion following the sensitivity practice recommended by @grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019 for composite indicators. Ranking stability is assessed by perturbing each criterion weight by plus or minus 20 percentage points, with remaining weights adjusted proportionally, and measuring rank-order correlations between the baseline ranking and each perturbed variant using Kendall's $\tau$. This constitutes an analytical evaluation of the artefact in the sense of @hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004 Guideline 3, which requires that the utility and quality of a design artefact be rigorously demonstrated via well-executed evaluation methods. A consistently high $\tau$ across perturbations provides evidence that the ranking is not sensitive to reasonable variation in weight assumptions, which is a necessary condition for the framework to be actionable in practice.

## Ethical and Professional Considerations

The ethical considerations arising from this study concern data confidentiality and legal compliance, the responsible use of prioritisation outputs, and alignment with professional standards.

### Data Confidentiality and Anonymisation

The data originate from a live Swiss financial institution that is not identified by name. All service, team, and vendor identifiers were replaced with alphanumeric codes before analysis. No employee data are present: all four indicators are service-level aggregates that cannot be attributed to individuals. The data-sharing arrangement was established with the explicit agreement of the Head of Trading IT, access was limited to the required records, and no data were retained beyond the analytical period or shared with third parties.

### Legal Compliance

The organisation is subject to the revised Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP), which entered into force on 1 September 2023 [@FederalActData2023], enforced by the Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC). Because the dataset contains no personal data, the nFADP's provisions on personal data processing do not apply directly. The principle of data minimisation, which the nFADP enshrines in alignment with international standards, was nonetheless observed: only the metrics required for the four TOPSIS indicators were extracted.

As a systemically significant institution, the organisation is also subject to FINMA Circular 2023/1 on operational risks and resilience, in force since 1 January 2024 [@finmaCircular202312024]. This circular requires comprehensive ICT operational risk management, including incident monitoring and service resilience. Areas structurally aligned with the framework's operational focus. Although the specific metrics used here are organisationally defined rather than prescribed by FINMA. No ethics committee approval was required under the University of Essex's research ethics framework, and a departmental no-objection confirmation was obtained.

### Responsible Use of Prioritisation Outputs

A relative rank assigned to a service carries a risk of being misused to evaluate team performance. High-debt service profiles typically reflect historical design decisions or legacy constraints rather than the competence of the team currently managing the service [@ahmadTechnicalDebtNot2026]. Three mitigations address this risk: framework documentation explicitly states that $C_i$ reflects the operational state of a service rather than team performance; outputs are intended for IT governance and portfolio management functions only; and the sensitivity analysis makes weighting assumptions transparent, reducing the risk of false precision.

### Professional Standards and Limitations

This study was conducted in accordance with the BCS Code of Conduct [@CodeConductBCS2022], including the obligations to act with integrity, to respect the rights of third parties, and to ensure that the artefact is fit for purpose. Three limitations clarify scope. The single-site design means that indicator weights and TOPSIS configuration are calibrated for one organisation and should not be transferred without revalidation. The cross-sectional window means the framework captures a snapshot; recently remediated services may be under-ranked. Finally, the four indicators do not exhaust all dimensions of operational technical debt; indicators related to documentation, test coverage, or architectural coupling were excluded due to data availability and remain candidates for future iterations.
